Page.Width: {Binding RelativeSource={RelativeSource Mode=FindAncestor, AncestorType={x:Type ContentControl}}, Path=ActualWidth, FallbackValue=700}
Page.Height: {Binding RelativeSource={RelativeSource Mode=FindAncestor, AncestorType={x:Type ContentControl}}, Path=ActualHeight, FallbackValue=700}

Run.Text: Now that we are deploying the towed array, let's cover the 'thermal layer' and its properties.

In most areas of the ocean, a distinct 'thermocline' boundary exists at a certain depth where the water temperature changes dramatically. This is referred to as the 'layer' when discussing sonar and acoustic detection of submarines. Submarines 'below the layer' will be harder to detect for surface ships and sonobuoys above the layer, because water temperature greatly affects sound travel through water.

Note that all shipboard sonar systems with the exception of the towed sonar arrays are only capable of listening above the layer! Aircraft-deployed sonobuoys can be set either above or below, but more on that later.
